question: A 62-year-old man with hypertension and diabetes presents with acute substernal chest pressure for 90 minutes. BP 150/92, HR 104. ECG shows ST elevation of 3mm in V1-V4 with reciprocal ST depression in II, III, aVF. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Aspirin 324mg chewed + ticagrelor 180mg + heparin + activate cath lab for primary PCI
Aspirin + nitroglycerin infusion + admit for stress testing in the morning
Administer tenecteplase immediately since patient is within thrombolytic window
CT angiogram to rule out aortic dissection before antiplatelet therapy
Aspirin + clopidogrel + admit to CCU for catheterization tomorrow morning

explanation.why_correct: Classic anterior STEMI requires immediate dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor), anticoagulation (heparin), and emergent PCI. Door-to-balloon goal ≤90 minutes. Primary PCI preferred over thrombolytics when available within 120 minutes due to superior reperfusion rates (TIMI 3 flow 90-95% vs 50-60%), lower intracranial hemorrhage risk (0.1% vs 1%), and reduced re-occlusion.

why_wrong.b: STEMI with ST elevation mandates emergent reperfusion, not observation and delayed stress testing. Every 30-minute delay increases 1-year mortality 7.5%.
why_wrong.c: Thrombolytics are indicated when PCI cannot be performed within 120 minutes. PCI is available here and preferred - lower ICH, higher reperfusion rates, better outcomes.
why_wrong.d: This presentation is classic STEMI, not dissection (no tearing pain, pulse deficit, or widened mediastinum). CT would delay life-saving reperfusion.
why_wrong.e: Delaying PCI to next morning is inappropriate. Myocardial necrosis progresses with time - salvage decreases, mortality increases.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Time is myocardium. Door-to-balloon ≤90min for PCI. If PCI unavailable within 120min, door-to-needle ≤30min for thrombolytics. Every 30-minute delay increases 1-year mortality 7.5%.

question: A 28-year-old woman with no medical history presents with sharp pleuritic chest pain for 24 hours, worse lying flat, better sitting forward. URI 1 week ago. T 38.1°C, BP 110/70, HR 98. Pericardial friction rub present. ECG: diffuse concave-up ST elevation in I, II, III, aVF, V2-V6 with PR depression in II. No reciprocal changes. Troponin I 2.5 ng/mL. Diagnosis and treatment?

options.text: Acute pericarditis; ibuprofen 600mg TID + colchicine 0.5mg BID for 3 months
STEMI; aspirin, heparin, emergent catheterization
Myocarditis; prednisone 60mg daily with rapid taper
Pulmonary embolism; enoxaparin + CT pulmonary angiography
Costochondritis; reassurance and acetaminophen PRN

explanation.why_correct: Classic acute pericarditis: pleuritic positional pain + friction rub + diffuse ST elevation without reciprocal changes + PR depression + elevated troponin (myopericarditis). Viral etiology likely post-URI. NSAIDs first-line + colchicine reduces recurrence 50% (COPE/ICAP trials). Add PPI for gastric protection.

why_wrong.b: Diffuse ST elevation without reciprocal changes = pericarditis, not STEMI. STEMI has localized ST elevation with reciprocal depression. Pleuritic positional pain is characteristic of pericarditis.
why_wrong.c: While myocarditis can present similarly, the friction rub and classic ECG favor pericarditis. Steroids NOT first-line - increase recurrence. Reserve for refractory cases.
why_wrong.d: PE presents with dyspnea, hypoxia, right heart strain (S1Q3T3), not diffuse ST elevation or friction rub. CT angiography not indicated without PE suspicion.
why_wrong.e: Costochondritis has reproducible chest wall tenderness, no ECG changes, no elevated troponin, no friction rub. These findings are specific for pericarditis.

explanation.clinical_pearl: Colchicine reduces pericarditis recurrence from 32% to 10% (COPE trial). ECG stages: 1-diffuse ST elevation+PR depression, 2-pseudonormalization, 3-diffuse T inversion, 4-normalization.

question: A 72-year-old woman with hypertension, diabetes, and chronic kidney disease (baseline Cr 1.8) presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea, and bilateral leg edema over 3 weeks. BP 170/95, HR 92, SpO2 91%. JVD 8cm, bibasilar crackles, S3 gallop. Echo: LVEF 40%, moderate mitral regurgitation, left atrial enlargement. BNP 1800 pg/mL. Which medication class has the BEST evidence for reducing mortality in this patient?

options.text: Sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) titrated to maximum tolerated dose, because it reduces mortality compared to enalapril in HFrEF
Furosemide 80mg IV BID to achieve aggressive diuresis and symptom relief
Digoxin 0.125mg daily to improve cardiac contractility and reduce heart failure hospitalizations
Metoprolol succinate 25mg daily started immediately to reduce heart rate and myocardial oxygen demand
Amlodipine 10mg daily to control blood pressure and reduce afterload

explanation.why_correct: This patient has HFrEF (EF 40%) with acute decompensation. Sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) demonstrated 20% reduction in cardiovascular death or HF hospitalization compared to enalapril in the PARADIGM-HF trial. GDMT for HFrEF includes ARNI/ACEi/ARB + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i (4 pillars). ARNI should be initiated after hemodynamic stabilization.

why_wrong.b: Furosemide is essential for decongestion but does NOT reduce mortality. It provides symptom relief only. GDMT reduces mortality; diuretics do not.
why_wrong.c: Digoxin reduces HF hospitalizations but does NOT reduce mortality (DIG trial). Used for symptom control in patients already on optimal GDMT with persistent symptoms.
why_wrong.d: Beta-blockers are essential GDMT but should be initiated when euvolemic, not during acute decompensation. Starting during decompensation can worsen symptoms.
why_wrong.e: Amlodipine is a vasodilator but has no mortality benefit in HFrEF. Not part of GDMT. May be used for persistent hypertension but not as primary HF therapy.

explanation.clinical_pearl: GDMT for HFrEF (4 pillars): ARNI/ACEi/ARB + Beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i. PARADIGM-HF: ARNI 20% reduction in CV death/HF hospitalization vs enalapril. Start beta-blockers when euvolemic. SGLT2i (Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin) reduce mortality in HFrEF regardless of diabetes status.

question: A 75-year-old man with hypertension and prior stroke presents with palpitations and fatigue for 2 weeks. Examination: irregularly irregular pulse, HR 115. ECG: absent P waves, irregular RR intervals, narrow QRS. He is hemodynamically stable. What is the MOST important factor in determining whether to start anticoagulation?

options.text: CHA₂DS₂-VASc score, because it estimates annual stroke risk and guides anticoagulation decision
Left atrial size on echocardiogram, because larger atria have higher thrombus risk
Duration of atrial fibrillation symptoms, because AF >48 hours requires TEE before cardioversion
Ventricular rate on presentation, because rapid rates indicate higher embolic risk
Serum troponin level, because elevated troponin indicates myocardial damage and higher stroke risk

explanation.why_correct: This patient has atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score estimates annual stroke risk: CHF (1), HTN (1), Age ≥75 (2), DM (1), Stroke/TIA (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Sex female (1). This patient: HTN (1) + Age ≥75 (2) + Prior Stroke (2) = score 5. Score ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women) = anticoagulation recommended. DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban) are first-line over warfarin.

why_wrong.b: LA size is associated with AF but does NOT directly guide anticoagulation. CHA₂DS₂-VASc is the evidence-based risk stratification tool.
why_wrong.c: Duration guides cardioversion timing (TEE vs 3 weeks anticoagulation), NOT the initial anticoagulation decision. All patients with CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 need anticoagulation regardless of AF duration.
why_wrong.d: Ventricular rate guides rate control therapy (beta-blockers, CCBs), not anticoagulation. Embolic risk is determined by comorbidities, not heart rate.
why_wrong.e: Troponin can be elevated in AF due to supply-demand mismatch, but does NOT independently guide anticoagulation. CHA₂DS₂-VASc remains the standard risk assessment.

explanation.clinical_pearl: CHA₂DS₂-VASc: CHF(1), HTN(1), Age≥75(2), DM(1), Stroke/TIA(2), Vascular(1), Age 65-74(1), Sex female(1). Score 0: no anticoagulation. Score 1: consider. Score ≥2: anticoagulation. DOACs preferred over warfarin (better safety, no INR monitoring). HAS-BLED for bleeding risk.
